framework_description: The 4S Framework categorizes simulation models into four increasing levels of capability that allow organizations and researchers to understand how far a simulation model has progressed in its ability to represent, analyze, predict, and support decisions about a system. The framework was developed as an analogous structure to the 4R Digital Twin Framework so that simulation and digital twin capabilities can be compared more clearly. Its purpose is to provide a capability-based classification for simulation models and reduce confusion between simulations and digital twins, especially in cases where advanced simulation models are incorrectly labeled as digital twins.
distinction_from_digital_twin: The 4S Framework is especially important because a high-capability simulation is still distinct from a digital twin unless it includes digital twin capabilities such as real-time data integration, synchronization with the physical system, bidirectional data flow, and autonomous or semi-autonomous feedback. A simulation can reach S4 by prescribing an optimal solution while still relying on historical data, offline experimentation, or one-way data flow.

levels.level_summary: Modeling is the first level of simulation capability. At this level, the simulation model creates an abstracted representation of a real-world or conceptual system. The goal is to understand the system, map its processes, identify its components, and convert the system’s behavior into a conceptual and computer model.
levels.key_check: Can the model explain the basic structure and behavior of the system? Can someone look at the model and understand what components exist, how entities flow, and what logic governs the process? If not, the simulation has not reached a useful Modeling level.

how_to_achieve_level.step_title: Define the purpose and scope of the simulation model

substeps.description: Identify why the simulation is being created and what system, process, object, or event it will represent. The purpose should explain whether the simulation is being used to understand flow, represent behavior, support a decision, test a layout, or evaluate system logic.
substeps.example: The simulation was created to model pallet movement, elevator movement, Head Buffer behavior, Head Return behavior, and Head Load B behavior within a bounded section of the manufacturing system.

substeps.description: Define the system boundary. Identify where the simulated system starts and ends, which machines, conveyors, resources, entities, and process interactions are included, and which parts are excluded.
substeps.example: The case study did not model the entire factory. It focused on the pallet elevator area and related components that affected pallet movement through that section.

substeps.description: Identify the entities, resources, events, queues, constraints, inputs, outputs, and operating logic. This is where the modeler determines what actually moves through the system, what processes it, what delays it, and what rules govern its behavior.
substeps.example: The pallet elevator logic involved checking elevator position, determining whether destination space was available, and deciding whether to move the pallet, adjust elevator position, or move without a pallet.

substeps.description: Identify the level of abstraction. Simulation models simplify reality. The modeler must decide which system details are necessary and which details can be simplified without harming the purpose of the model.
substeps.example: The pallet elevator model represented the critical machines, conveyors, sources, sink, and vehicle behavior rather than every minor physical detail of the plant.

substeps.description: Map the process flow. Show how entities move through the system, where they wait, where they are processed, where decisions occur, and where they exit the model.
substeps.example: The flow included pallet arrivals, Head Buffer, Head Install, Head Return, the elevator, and Head Load B.

substeps.description: Define assumptions and simplifications. These assumptions explain how the real system is translated into a manageable simulation model.
substeps.example: The baseline simulation represented the system using three sources, three servers, one vehicle, and one sink.

substeps.description: Select the simulation approach and software. Common approaches include Discrete Event Simulation, Monte Carlo simulation, and Agent-Based simulation. DES is especially common in manufacturing because it can represent event-based system behavior over time.
substeps.example: The case study used DES in Simio. Source1 simulated pallet arrivals, Source2 and Source3 initialized pallets, servers represented Head Install and Head Load B, and the elevator was modeled as a vehicle.

substeps.description: Translate the conceptual model into virtual objects. Each major physical or logical system component should have a corresponding simulation element.
substeps.example: Physical elements such as conveyors, processing machines, and the pallet elevator were translated into simulation objects.

levels.level_summary: Analyzing is the second level of simulation capability. At this level, the simulation model is used to examine the system and draw conclusions about its behavior. The model incorporates data, uses input analysis, and is verified and validated against the system being modeled.
levels.key_check: Can the model be used to explain why the system behaves a certain way? Are the inputs reasonable, the model verified, the behavior validated, and the results interpretable? If the model only exists visually but cannot support analysis, it is still closer to S1 than S2.

substeps.description: Identify required input data. This can include arrival times, processing times, travel times, failure data, repair times, conveyor speeds, routing rules, capacities, and resource availability.
substeps.example: The case study used timestamp data, downtime data, processing times, conveyor information, CAD files, and engineer input.

substeps.description: Classify the data and determine how it will be used. Data may support arrival logic, processing logic, resource behavior, reliability logic, or routing logic.
substeps.example: Timestamps supported pallet arrival logic. Downtime data supported reliability modeling for Head Install and Head Load B.

substeps.description: Fit distributions or define empirical patterns. The modeler should determine whether the data should be represented by distributions, empirical tables, fixed values, or logic rules.
substeps.example: Downtime data were analyzed to determine appropriate distributions for time between failures and repair times.

substeps.description: Check that the simulation logic works as intended. This includes checking routing, entity movement, resource logic, timing, constraints, and decision rules.
substeps.example: The pallet elevator logic was checked during development to make sure the simulated elevator followed the intended movement and pallet-handling rules.

substeps.description: Compare model behavior with real system behavior or expert knowledge. Validation may use observed data, historical data, SME review, or known system outputs.
substeps.example: The case study used real system logic, historical plant data, layout information, and engineer input to support model validity.

substeps.description: Define performance measures. Common measures include throughput, wait time, queue length, utilization, idle time, travel time, blocking, starvation, cost, or cycle time.
substeps.example: The case study tracked elevator movement time without pallet, elevator idle time, and Head Load B starved time.

substeps.description: Run experiments or parameter changes. The model can be used to examine how the system behaves under selected settings.
substeps.example: The baseline model was used to understand current-state behavior before moving into prediction and optimization.

levels.level_summary: Predicting is the third level of simulation capability. At this level, the simulation model can predict how the physical system may operate under new or different conditions. It supports what-if scenarios, alternative configurations, and performance comparison.
levels.key_check: Can the simulation test a realistic alternative condition and forecast how the system would behave? Can it compare that predicted behavior against the baseline? If it only explains current behavior, it is S2. If it tests future or alternative behavior, it reaches S3.

substeps.description: Identify what future or alternative behavior needs to be tested. This could include a new operating rule, different capacity, changed layout, altered demand level, new equipment, or modified resource logic.
substeps.example: The study tested whether Alternative Elevator Logic could reduce starvation and improve system performance.

substeps.description: Modify model inputs, structure, or logic to represent a new condition. Prediction is stronger when the scenario changes something meaningful about how the system operates.
substeps.example: The Alternative Elevator Logic changed the decision-making behavior of the elevator.

substeps.description: Ensure the scenario is realistic and feasible. Scenarios should be reviewed against system constraints and domain knowledge.
substeps.example: The Alternative Elevator Logic threshold was developed with input from area engineers and considered safe by area managers.

how_to_achieve_level.step_title: Run the scenario and compare it to baseline

substeps.description: Use the same performance measures where possible. This allows the modeler to determine whether the new scenario improves, worsens, or has little effect on system performance.
substeps.example: The Alternative Elevator Logic was compared to the baseline using performance measures such as Head Install starved time, elevator movement without pallet, and other system measures.

substeps.description: Determine whether the predicted result supports or rejects the expected improvement.
substeps.example: The Alternative Elevator Logic negatively impacted all performance measures in the initial comparison. The small increase in Head Install starved time challenged the idea that changing the elevator logic would reduce starvation.

levels.level_summary: Prescribing is the fourth level of simulation capability. At this level, the simulation model is used for decision support and optimization. The model evaluates possible alternatives and provides insight into the best course of action, optimal operation, or preferred configuration.
levels.key_check: Can the simulation recommend the best course of action based on defined objectives, constraints, and alternatives? If it only compares scenarios, it is S3. If it identifies or recommends an optimal configuration, it reaches S4.

how_to_achieve_level.step_title: Define the decision or optimization problem

substeps.description: Identify the objective function. The objective function defines what the simulation is trying to minimize, maximize, or improve.
substeps.example: The objective was to minimize Head Install starved time.

substeps.description: Identify decision variables. These are the controllable settings or configurations that the simulation can change during the optimization process.
substeps.example: The Head Buffer conveyor capacity was used as the control variable.

substeps.description: Identify constraints and feasible ranges. Optimization should stay within realistic physical, operational, or safety limits.
substeps.example: The Head Buffer conveyor capacity ranged from 1 to 17 pallets, while the Head Return capacity was set as the remaining capacity.

substeps.description: Use simulation optimization, machine learning, or another decision-support technique.
substeps.example: The case study used the OptQuest add-in in Simio.

substeps.description: Compare candidate configurations using the objective function and performance measures.
substeps.example: The optimization was run for both the Baseline Model and the Alternative Elevator Logic model.

how_to_achieve_level.step_title: Prescribe the preferred configuration or action

substeps.description: Identify the best-performing option and explain the expected benefit.
substeps.example: The optimal setting for both the Baseline and Alternative Elevator Logic models was a Head Buffer capacity of 17 pallets and a Head Return capacity of 1 pallet. Under the optimized settings, the Alternative Elevator Logic model performed slightly better than the optimized Baseline model.

substeps.description: Note that prescribing does not automatically mean autonomous implementation. A simulation may recommend an optimal solution, but that recommendation is still usually applied by a human decision-maker unless the model is connected to a real-time autonomous control loop.
substeps.example: This is why an S4 simulation is still not automatically an R4 digital twin. The simulation can prescribe an action, but DT capability depends on real-time connection, synchronization, bidirectional data flow, and autonomy.
